chemistry.mechanism_type: Hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) followed by electrochemical oxidation
chemistry.scope_summary: Various pyrazoles, triazoles, tetrazoles, purines coupled with cyclic ethers, linear ethers, sulfur-containing compounds, nitrogen-containing compounds, and benzylic positions. 23 examples with yields ranging from 23-95%.

optimization_table.key_findings: Entry 1 (standard conditions) gave optimal 88% yield with 0.3 mmol scale, 15 min residence time

components: adjustable spacer with serpentine flow channels
figures.key_information: Flow electrophotocatalysis (f-EPC) reactor with photons and electrons in the same reactor, robust design, modular setup, interchangeable components, solvent resistant materials, user-friendly
figures.caption: Scheme 1.  A) Flow electrophotocatalysis ( f -EPC). B) Previous examples of flow electrophotocatalysis. C) Electrophotocatalytic C(sp 3 ) – H hetero- arylation via the merger of photocatalysis and electrocatalysis enabled by a newly fabricated  f -EPC reactor.

figures.key_information: Electrochemical flow reactor with transparent electrodes and adjustable PTFE spacer for precise electrode gap control
figures.caption: Scheme 2.  A) Design and picture of the newly constructed  f -EPC reactor. B) Validation of the flow reactor for electrochemical- and photochemical-only reactions.

figures.flow_path: single flow path through undivided cell
figures.key_information: Electrochemical flow reactor with Pt/FTO electrode, 52 W LED (λ = 390 nm) illumination, flow rate = 0.08 mL·min⁻¹, current I = 77 mA, current density j = 3.2 mA·cm⁻², residence time tR = 15 min
figures.caption: Scheme 2.  A) Design and picture of the newly constructed  f -EPC reactor. B) Validation of the flow reactor for electrochemical- and photochemical-only reactions.

Scheme 3.  Substrate scope for the electrophotocatalytic C(sp 3 ) – H heteroarylation in flow. Heteroarene (0.3 mmol), hydrogen donor (6 equiv), FeCl 3 (5 mol%), Et 4 NCl (1 equiv), AcOH (10 equiv) in CH 3 CN (0.1 M, 3 mL); solution sparged with N 2  prior to irradiation. Flow setup: V R = 1.2 mL, flow rate = 0.08 mL · min – 1 , t R = 15 min, 52 W LED ( λ = 390 nm), undivided cell: C anode/Pt j FT

key_observations_supporting_mechanism: TEMPO trapping experiments showing radical intermediates
Kinetic isotope effect (KIE = 1.02) indicating C-H bond breaking is not rate-determining
Competition experiments with THF and THF-d8
Formation of deuterated products in parallel reactions
figures.caption: Scheme 4.  Mechanistic investigations into the electrophotocatalytic C(sp 3 ) – H heteroarylation. A) Radical trapping experiments. B) Kinetic isotope effects: Standard conditions: [a]  1a  (0.3 mmol),  2a  (6 equiv), FeCl 3  (5 mol%), Et 4 NCl (1 equiv), AcOH (10 equiv) in CH 3 CN (0.1 M, 3 mL); solution sparged with N 2  prior to irradiation. Flow setup: V R = 1.2 mL, flow rate = 0.08 mL · min –

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Longer reaction times (240 minutes vs 15 minutes), lower yield (80% vs 88%)
translation_logic.flow_advantage: 16-fold reduction in reaction time, improved yield, continuous operation with better heat and mass transfer
translation_logic.what_changed: Residence time reduced from 240 min to 15 min, reactor design changed to continuous flow electrophotocatalytic reactor
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Solvent (CH3CN), concentration (0.1 M), light source power (52 W), catalyst system (FeCl3)
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Continuous flow operation with better heat transfer and reduced reaction volume

figure_descriptions: Multiple reactor schemes showing f-EPC reactor design with transparent electrodes and serpentine flow channels
Scope table with 22 examples showing substrate tolerance
Mechanistic schemes with radical trapping and kinetic isotope effect studies

conflicts: Pass 2 chemistry scope mentions 23 examples but Pass 4 scope table shows 22 examples
notes: Well-documented flow electrophotocatalysis study with clear batch-to-flow translation, comprehensive reactor design information, and thorough mechanistic investigation. Minor conflict on number of scope examples.
